content.text: A language model's pre-training objective is next-token prediction on raw text. It has no inherent concept of roles, turns, or the distinction between system instructions and user queries.

is the layer that imposes structure: a deterministic function that converts a list of role-tagged messages into a single flat token sequence, delimited by special tokens the model was trained to recognise as role boundaries.

Getting the template wrong at inference time — even by a single token or missing delimiter — causes the model to misinterpret role boundaries. The symptoms range from subtle (ignoring the system prompt) to severe (outputting a continuation of the prompt rather than a response). Critically,
every model family has its own template
, and templates are not interchangeable across model families.

attrs.alt: Chat formatting diagram: message list converted to Llama-3 and ChatML token sequences, with Jinja2 template internals and loss masking colour-coding

content.text: Every chat template encodes the same four structural elements, just with different tokens:
— a unique token or string placed once at the very beginning (e.g.
— marks the start of a turn and declares which role is speaking (system, user, or assistant).
— the raw text of that turn, with no special tokens inside it.
— a delimiter that signals the turn is complete (e.g.
Used by Meta's Llama-3 family (8B, 70B, 405B Instruct). Verbose header tokens make role boundaries unambiguous even without a system prompt.

<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>

You are a helpful assistant.<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

What is an LSTM?<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>

An LSTM is...<|eot_id|>
Originally designed by OpenAI, adopted by Qwen, Phi-3-mini, Mistral-7B v0.3+, and many community fine-tunes. Compact and widely supported.

<|im_start|>system
You are a helpful assistant.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
What is an LSTM?<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
An LSTM is...<|im_end|>
Older format; note that the system prompt is embedded
block rather than as a separate role.

<s>[INST] <<SYS>>
You are a helpful assistant.
<</SYS>>

What is an LSTM? [/INST] An LSTM is... </s><s>[INST] Follow-up? [/INST]
to apply the correct template for whatever model you have loaded, without any hard-coding.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

messages = [
    {"role": "system",    "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "What is an LSTM?"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "An LSTM is a type of recurrent network..."},
]

# --- Inference: get the prompt ready for generation ---
prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    messages,
    tokenize=False,
    add_generation_prompt=True,  # appends the opening assistant header
)
# Tokenise and send to the model
input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids

# --- Training: full conversation, no extra generation header ---
full_text = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    messages,
    tokenize=False,
    add_generation_prompt=False,  # include the closing <|eot_id|>
)
Set False when you want the string to inspect or pass to a custom tokeniser
Set True at inference; the model needs the open assistant header to start generating
Set 'pt' or 'tf' when tokenize=True and you want a tensor directly
Set True when processing a batch of messages with varying lengths
Truncates to this length when tokenize=True; truncation happens from left (removes old turns)
Each model's template is stored as a Jinja2 string in
You can customise the template for your fine-tune by modifying the Jinja2 string and saving it with
. This is useful when you want to inject a fixed system prompt during training, add a tool-call format, or strip tokens the base model did not have.
During inference, multi-turn dialogue is built incrementally by appending each new turn to the message list and re-applying the template. The model's context window holds all prior turns:

conversation = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
]

def chat(user_input: str, conversation: list, model, tokenizer, max_new_tokens=256):
    conversation.append({"role": "user", "content": user_input})
    input_ids = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        conversation,
        return_tensors="pt",
        add_generation_prompt=True,
    ).to(model.device)
    output_ids = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens)
    # Only decode the new tokens (skip re-decoding the prompt)
    new_tokens = output_ids[0, input_ids.shape[1]:]
    response = tokenizer.decode(new_tokens, skip_special_tokens=True)
    conversation.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response})
    return response, conversation

response, conversation = chat("What is an LSTM?", conversation, model, tokenizer)
print(response)
As the conversation grows, the token count grows with it. When the total exceeds
, you must truncate. The safe strategy is to drop the oldest user-assistant turns while keeping the system prompt intact.
Models that support function calling (tool use) extend the chat format with additional roles or structured content fields. Llama-3.1+ defines a
role and encodes tool calls as JSON inside the assistant turn:
argument — check the model card. When a model does not have a tool-call template, you must encode the function schema and results as plain text in the system or user role.
The template produces a slightly different sequence depending on whether you are training or running inference:
Closing EOS/eot token after the last assistant turn
Full sequence present; compute loss on assistant tokens
During training, the labels array mirrors the token ids but with
for every token that belongs to the system or user turns. PyTorch's cross-entropy loss ignores
tokens, so gradients flow only through the assistant tokens. A typical implementation computes the prompt length in tokens, then sets
Applying the Template to a Dataset
For SFT, apply the template during dataset pre-processing rather than inside the training loop to avoid re-computing it for every epoch:

from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

dataset = load_dataset("HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k", split="train_sft")

def preprocess(example):
    # example["messages"] is a list of {role, content} dicts
    full = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        example["messages"],
        tokenize=True,
        add_generation_prompt=False,
        max_length=2048,
        truncation=True,
        padding="max_length",
    )
    # Compute prompt length to determine the masking boundary
    prompt_msgs = example["messages"][:-1]  # everything except last assistant turn
    prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        prompt_msgs,
        tokenize=True,
        add_generation_prompt=True,  # include opening assistant header in prompt
    )
    labels = [-100] * len(prompt) + full[len(prompt):]
    labels = labels[:2048] + [-100] * (2048 - len(labels))  # pad labels too
    return {"input_ids": full, "labels": labels, "attention_mask": [1]*len(full)}

tokenized_dataset = dataset.map(preprocess, remove_columns=dataset.column_names)
Model echoes the prompt, ignores system, or outputs garbage
Use apply_chat_template — it reads the stored Jinja2 template for the model you loaded
Model doesn't know it should start the reply; generates user content instead
Always set True for inference; False for training
Prompt ends with EOS; model generates nothing or a single token
Verify the formatted string does not end with a closing EOS at inference time
Performance degrades vs expected; model trained with one format, served with another
Pin the template in tokenizer_config.json; save and load the same tokenizer everywhere
RuntimeError during batched tokenisation with padding=True
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token before any batched call
Role boundary tokens in user content confuse the model
Sanitise user input; strip or escape model-specific special token strings before passing to the template
When something seems off, print the fully formatted string and count tokens explicitly:

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role": "user",   "content": "Hello!"},
]

# 1. Print the raw formatted string
formatted = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True)
print(repr(formatted))   # repr() shows escape sequences, special tokens clearly

# 2. Check token count
tokens = tokenizer(formatted, return_tensors="pt")
print("Token count:", tokens.input_ids.shape[1])

# 3. Decode each token individually to inspect boundaries
for idx, tok_id in enumerate(tokens.input_ids[0]):
    print(f"{idx:3d}: {tok_id:6d}  '{tokenizer.decode([tok_id])}'")
Every model family defines its own chat template — a Jinja2 string stored in tokenizer_config.json that maps role-tagged messages to a flat token sequence.
Use tokenizer.apply_chat_template() for all formatting; never hand-build the string yourself.
Set add_generation_prompt=True at inference so the model knows to generate an assistant reply; set it False during training so the closing EOS token is included in the sequence.
Loss masking (labels = -100 for prompt tokens) ensures only assistant tokens update weights during SFT.
Inspect and customise the Jinja2 template to inject fixed system prompts, add tool-call schemas, or adapt the format for your fine-tune.
Pin the tokenizer (and its chat_template) alongside the model weights; a mismatched template at serving time silently degrades quality.
